step_by_step_reasoning: We trace the architecture chronologically. A developer requests a new replica environment via the Orion portal. The request resolves via Route 53, passes through the Application Load Balancer (ALB), and hits the Orion application pod running on Amazon EKS. The application triggers EKS Jobs on EC2 Spot Instances to provision 70+ microservices. To ensure resiliency during Spot terminations, these jobs send status events to SNS, which queues them in SQS. New/replacement jobs poll SQS to resume interrupted tasks. The orchestration status returns to the developer.

graph.name: OLX Autos: Scaling to 10k+ Pods on 100% Spot Instances
graph.notes: OLX Autos manages replica development environments on an internal platform. They achieve high cost optimization by running 100% Spot instances, utilizing AWS Node Termination Handler alongside SNS/SQS to safely resume disrupted Kubernetes jobs.

nodes.name: Developer
nodes.notes: Triggers environment replication requests on the Orion portal.

nodes.name: Route 53
nodes.notes: Provides DNS resolution for the Orion portal.

nodes.name: Application Load Balancer
nodes.notes: Routes portal requests to application pods.

nodes.name: Amazon EKS
nodes.notes: WORKLOAD_PEEK: Handles 10,000+ pods. Orchestrates the microservice replication process.

nodes.name: Spot Instances
nodes.notes: WORKLOAD_PEEK: 100% Spot-backed node groups running transient Kubernetes jobs to deploy microservices.

nodes.name: Amazon SNS
nodes.notes: Receives tracking state events when microservice jobs are triggered.

nodes.name: Amazon SQS
nodes.notes: Buffers job progress states, ensuring outstanding work is resumed if nodes terminate.

edges.notes: Forwards request to Orion app on EKS
Launches jobs on EC2 Spot instances to provision microservices
Publishes task tracking state on execution
Fans out task states to queue
Replacement jobs read pending states from SQS queue
